questions.questionText: Enzymes increase the rate of chemical reactions by:
questions.optionC: Increasing the free energy of products
questions.explanation: Enzymes stabilize the transition state, reducing the energy barrier.

questions.optionC: Affinity of the enzyme for its inhibitor
questions.explanation: Km is inversely related to the enzyme's affinity for its substrate.

questions.optionA: Vmax is decreased, Km is unchanged
questions.optionB: Vmax is unchanged, Km is increased
questions.optionC: Both Vmax and Km are increased
questions.optionD: Both Vmax and Km are decreased
questions.explanation: The inhibitor competes for the active site; high substrate can overcome it.

questions.optionA: Vmax is unchanged, Km is increased
questions.optionB: Both Vmax and Km are decreased
questions.optionC: Both Vmax and Km are increased
questions.optionD: Vmax is decreased, Km is unchanged
questions.explanation: The inhibitor binds somewhere other than the active site, reducing functional enzyme concentration.

questions.questionText: Allosteric enzymes typically exhibit a kinetics curve that is:
questions.explanation: Cooperativity between subunits causes a sigmoidal velocity vs. substrate curve.

questions.questionText: An apoenzyme combined with its necessary cofactor is termed a(n):
questions.explanation: Holoenzyme refers to the active, complete enzyme-cofactor complex.

questions.optionA: Enzymes involved in the same metabolic pathway
questions.optionC: Enzymes that require the same cofactor
questions.optionD: Enzymes that catalyze the same reaction but differ in structure and properties
questions.explanation: E.g., LDH has different isozymes in heart vs. muscle.

questions.questionText: Which of the following classes of enzymes catalyze the cleavage of bonds by the addition of water?
questions.explanation: Hydrolases (like digestive enzymes) use water to break bonds.

questions.questionText: The lineweaver-Burk plot (double reciprocal) yields a y-intercept that corresponds to:
Suicide inhibition is an example of:
questions.explanation: The enzyme converts the inhibitor into a reactive intermediate that covalently modifies the active site.

questions.questionText: In Enzymes, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Biochemistry texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Biochemistry references for Enzymes. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Enzymes.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Enzymes scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Enzymes.
